title: Subsidies, Stockpiles, and Shortages
gist: Three months into the Iran war, trade through the Strait of Hormuz is at a virtual halt; Kuwait has banned essential food exports while Qatar is flying produce from three continents to cap domestic food inflation at 5-10%, but Lebanon faces 1.24 million people at acute food insecurity risk, Egypt raised energy prices 31%, and Jordan paid $200M extra to import fuel it can barely afford.

themes.theme: Iran war commodity shock: Gulf and Levant emergency responses

insights.claim: Three months into the Iran war, trade through the Strait of Hormuz is at a 'virtual halt,' prompting Kuwait to impose a temporary ban on exports of essential food items—rice, cooking oil, and wheat flour—to prevent domestic shortages.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Strait of Hormuz closure is generating real commodity supply scarcity, not just price volatility; export bans within the Gulf itself signal stress has moved beyond price management.
insights.quote: trade through the Strait of Hormuz at a virtual halt
insights.locator: Three months after the start of the Iran war

insights.claim: Qatar, which imports 90% of its food supply, has flown in grains and fresh produce from Europe, Africa, and the Americas, limiting food price increases to 5-10% rather than potentially far larger spikes—demonstrating that wealthy Gulf states with emergency logistics can contain the shock.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Qatar's ability to sustain only 5-10% food inflation via air freight shows the supply chain resilience of wealthy Gulf states; for poorer Levant countries lacking this option, the humanitarian outcome is far worse.
insights.quote: Qatar, which imports 90 percent of its food supply, have implemented food subsidies and restructured their supply chains. The government has flown in grains and fresh produce from Europe, Africa, and the Americas to ease shortages, limiting cost increases to 5–10 percent
insights.locator: Other Gulf nations, such as Qatar

insights.claim: Lebanon, where 20% of the population has been displaced due to the war, faces 1.24 million people at risk of high levels of acute food insecurity between April and August 2026, per the Integrated Food Security Phase Classification.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Lebanon is approaching famine-level conditions for over a million people; humanitarian intervention requirements are acute and sovereign credit risk is extreme.
insights.quote: the IPC predicts that 1.24 million people in Lebanon could face high levels of acute food insecurity between April and August 2026
insights.locator: Lebanon, where 20 percent of the population

insights.claim: Egypt raised energy prices by 31% in April 2026; Jordan was forced to pay an additional $200 million to import fuel in March (95% fuel-import dependent)—both countries have introduced subsidies and rationing measures that often fall short of meeting vulnerable groups' needs.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Egypt and Jordan face acute fiscal stress from fuel/food subsidies meeting a simultaneous import cost shock; risk of IMF emergency programs and social unrest is elevated in both countries.
insights.quote: In Egypt, authorities increased energy prices by 31 percent in April, citing the global energy crisis and budget burdens due to the war. In March, the Jordanian government was forced to pay an additional $200 million to import fuel
insights.locator: In Egypt, authorities increased energy prices
